steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Laidre's argument rests on a conditional: if the tusk serves a critical purpose, then natural selection would make it widespread. Yet many narwhals lack a tusk — so the choice must state the evolutionary consequence that follows from a trait being truly important to survival.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — some narwhals would seek a new habitat.: describes an outcome unrelated to whether a critical trait spreads through a population. ✗
  • Choice B — more narwhals would have a tusk.: if a trait is critical for survival, natural selection favors it and it becomes common — so more narwhals would have the tusk. This is exactly the evolutionary logic Laidre invokes to challenge the explanation. ✓
  • Choice C — fewer marine animals would also have tusks.: describes an unrelated outcome that does not follow from a trait being survival-critical. ✗
  • Choice D — narwhals would become less shy over time.: similarly off-topic; does not address population-level spread of a beneficial trait. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
